_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — P2 Bezpieczne używanie AI
_meta.interaction: Rubryka weryfikacji odpowiedzi AI (kind: rubric, bez recordsPractical — ćwiczenie, nie bramka)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE przykłady SYNTETYCZNE (NotkaApp/SklepDemo, przyklad.test, Jan Syntetyczny). ZERO realnych danych/PII.

intro: A language model generates the most probable answer, not verified truth — and it always does so in the same confident tone. This module gives you a simple, repeatable verification method: source, date, second trace, limits of competence. At the end you will practice it on a concrete AI answer.

screens.title: Why AI sounds confident when it makes things up

blocks.text: The model does not “know” and does not “check” — it predicts the next words based on patterns from its training data. That is why truth and fabrication (hallucination) come out of it equally fluent, with equal flair. Tone, length, and a polished format are style, not evidence.
Information generated by the model that sounds credible but is false — a made-up fact, figure, quote, or source.

blocks.title: The most common trap
blocks.text: The model can also make up the SOURCE: the title of a report, an author, a link. The mere fact that an answer has a citation proves nothing — the citation has to be opened.

items: Identify the claims that carry a decision: figures, dates, names, quotes, recommendations.
Open the cited source: does it exist? does it actually contain this information?
Check the date: is the information current for your question?
Find a second, INDEPENDENT trace: another source confirming the key fact (not another answer from the same model).
Only then use the content further — with corrections, if verification turned anything up.

blocks.title: Independence
blocks.text: A second summary from the same chat, or asking “confirm this is true” in the same window, is NOT independent verification — it is the same machine and the same context.
Not every answer requires an investigation — a pancake recipe gets verified by the frying pan. You dial up vigilance in proportion to the cost of an error:

blocks.textAlt: Table of elevated-risk topics: health, law, finance, statistics, and news require verification in sources or with a specialist; the higher the cost of an error, the stronger the verification.

blocks.title: Knowledge cutoff
blocks.text: The model “stopped” at the end of its training data. Questions about “current” rates, regulations, or software versions are an invitation to an out-of-date answer delivered in a confident tone.

screens.title: The habit: verify before you pass it on

blocks.text: The greatest damage comes not from the hallucination itself but from its afterlife: pasted into an offer, sent to family, repeated in a meeting. Your rule: you do not pass on anything you cannot defend in your own words and trace back to where you know it from.

items: Got 10 minutes? Verify what carries the decision; explicitly mark the rest as unverified.
Someone asks “how do you know that?” — “from AI” is not enough; have a source.
Spotted a hallucination in someone else’s material? Say so — you break the chain.

blocks.title: The good news
blocks.text: This habit costs minutes, and it protects your credibility — the hardest resource to rebuild, at work and in the family.

interaction.title: Verify an AI answer against the rubric
interaction.intro: The AI generated a paragraph for a newsletter: “According to a report by the Synthetic Institute at przyklad.test, as many as 87% of companies in Poland have deployed AI in customer service. The figure has been growing for years. Full report: przyklad.test/raport-ai”. Assess this answer against the four verification criteria — choose the actions that genuinely verify.

criteria.name: Source: exists and confirms
criteria.prompt: What do you do with the cited source before you use the 87% figure?
criteria.hint: The citation has to be opened — the model can make up a report together with its link.

options.text: I open the link and check whether the report exists and whether it contains exactly this figure.
Since the link looks credible, I consider the source confirmed.
I ask the same AI whether the report really exists.

criteria.name: Date and currency
criteria.prompt: How do you check whether the statistic is current?
criteria.hint: “Growing for years” without a date is an empty claim — the model’s knowledge has a cutoff date.

options.text: I assume that since the AI answered today, the data is from today.
I look for the study date in the report and check whether newer data exists.
I decide that the trend “growing for years” is good enough instead of a date.

criteria.name: Second independent trace
criteria.prompt: What counts as independent confirmation of the 87% figure?
criteria.hint: Independent = a different machine/institution/publication, not the same chat.

options.text: A second answer from the same chat in which the AI “confirms” the figure.
The same figure pasted over by a colleague from the same tool.
Another credible source (for example, an independent industry publication) reporting a similar value.

criteria.name: Limits of competence and the decision
criteria.prompt: Verification showed that the report cannot be found. What do you do with the paragraph?
criteria.hint: An unverified figure does not go to publication — no matter how good it sounds.

options.text: I publish it unchanged — the paragraph sounds professional, and readers will not check anyway.
I remove the fabricated statistic; if the topic needs a figure, I look for one in an existing, checkable source and cite that source.
I keep the figure but remove the link so nobody discovers the report does not exist.

points: A confident tone, length, and format are the model’s style, not evidence — truth and hallucination sound identical.
Verification = an opened source + a date + a second independent trace; the same chat does not verify itself.
Health, law, money, statistics, and news — here you always verify, in proportion to the cost of an error.
checklist: Did I open the cited source and find the referenced information in it?
Do I know the date of the information and is it current for my use?
Do I have a second, independent trace for the key facts and figures?
Can I defend this content in my own words before I pass it on?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — before you use an AI answer
summary.nextStep: This is the last module of the path. Complete the required modules and take the final test — remember that the critical questions about sensitive data require a full set of correct answers.
